structuredFacts.title: Albert Einstein
structuredFacts.subtitle: German-born theoretical physicist (1879–1955)
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Albert Einstein (14 March 1879 – 18 April 1955) was a German-born theoretical physicist best known for developing the known theory of relativity. Einstein also made important contributions to quantum theory. His mass–energy equivalence formula E = mc2, which arises from special relativity, has been called "the world's most famous equation". He received the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for "his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect".
Born as a subject to the Kingdom of Württemberg, part of the German Empire, Einstein moved to Switzerland in 1895, forsaking his citizenship the following year. In 1896, at the age of seventeen, he enrolled in the mathematics and physics teaching diploma program at the Swiss federal polytechnic school in Zurich, graduating in 1900. He acquired Swiss citizenship a year later, which he kept for the rest of his life, and afterwards secured a permanent position at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern. In 1905, he submitted a successful PhD dissertation to the University of Zurich. In 1914, he moved to Berlin to join the Prussian Academy of Sciences and the Humboldt University of Berlin, becoming director of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Physics in 1917; he also became a Prussian and consequently also German citizen again. In 1933, while Einstein was visiting the United States, Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany. Horrified by the Nazi persecution of his fellow Jews, he decided to remain in the US, and was granted American citizenship in 1940. On the eve of World War II, he endorsed a letter to President Franklin D. Roosevelt alerting him to the potential German nuclear weapons program and recommending that the US begin similar research, later carried out as the Manhattan Project.
In 1905, sometimes described as his annus mirabilis (miracle year), he published four groundbreaking papers. In them, he outlined a theory of the photoelectric effect, explained Brownian motion, introduced his special theory of relativity, and demonstrated that if the special theory is correct, mass and energy are equivalent to each other. In 1915, he proposed a general theory of relativity that extended his system of mechanics to incorporate gravitation. A paper that he published the following year laid out the implications of general relativity for the modeling of the structure and evolution of the universe as a whole. It introduced the cosmological constant and is further regarded as the first step in the field of modern theoretical cosmology. In 1917, Einstein wrote a paper which introduced the concepts of spontaneous emission and stimulated emission, the latter of which is the core mechanism behind the laser and maser, and which contained a trove of information that would be beneficial to developments in physics later on, such as quantum electrodynamics and quantum optics.
In the middle part of his career, Einstein made important contributions to statistical mechanics and quantum theory. Especially notable was his work on the quantum physics of radiation, in which light consists of particles, subsequently called photons. With physicist Satyendra Nath Bose, he laid the groundwork for Bose–Einstein statistics. For much of the last phase of his academic life, Einstein worked on two endeavors that ultimately proved unsuccessful. First, he advocated against quantum theory's introduction of fundamental randomness into science's picture of the world, objecting that "God does not play dice". Second, he attempted to devise a unified field theory by generalizing his geometric theory of gravitation to include electromagnetism. As a result, he became increasingly isolated from mainstream modern physics. Many things are named after him, including the element Einsteinium. In 1999, he was named Time's Person of the Century.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Born in Ulm in 1879 to secular Jewish parents, Albert Einstein showed early mathematical genius. At just twelve years old, he discovered an original proof of the Pythagorean theorem entirely on his own, and by age fourteen, he had taught himself calculus. Yet his academic path was not without hurdles; at sixteen, he failed the general portion of the entrance exam for the Zurich polytechnic school. A year later, at age seventeen, the teenager renounced his German citizenship. Decades of work culminated in his 1905 Annus Mirabilis, during which he published four groundbreaking papers detailing the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and mass-energy equivalence. He then went on to develop the general theory of relativity in 1915.

cards.title: The Spark of Genius
cards.summary: In 1879, Albert Einstein was born to secular Jewish parents in Ulm, Germany . His innate intellectual curiosity drove him to independently study advanced mathematics and physics at a young age . At twelve years old, the young boy discovered an original proof of the Pythagorean theorem . This exceptional mathematical aptitude paved the way for his relocation to Switzerland and future scientific breakthroughs . At age sixteen, Einstein failed the general portion of his Zurich polytechnic entrance exam . Ultimately, his early mathematical genius overcame academic setbacks to launch a global scientific revolution .
cards.readerQuestion: How did a young boy from Germany develop his early mathematical genius?
cards.keyTakeaway: Early mathematical genius overcame academic setbacks to launch a revolution.

cards.title: The Swiss Patent Clerk
cards.summary: At just seventeen years old, Albert Einstein renounced his German citizenship in 1896, embarking on five years of statelessness before finally securing Swiss citizenship in 1901. Driven by a quest for academic freedom and personal independence, he severed his ties to Germany to immerse himself in classical philosophy and the vibrant intellectual life of Switzerland. This philosophical journey had actually begun at age thirteen, when he first read Immanuel Kant's Critique of Pure Reason, a text that quickly became his favorite work. Ultimately, these formative years in Switzerland provided the intellectual grounding and personal stability he needed to challenge established scientific paradigms. His personal life flourished there as well; his sister, Maja, married Paul Winteler, the son of the host family with whom Einstein had lodged and whose daughter, Marie, had been his first love. This unique blend of philosophical depth and Swiss independence ultimately nurtured the mind that would revolutionize physics.
cards.readerQuestion: How did an academic outcast revolutionize physics from a Swiss patent office?
cards.keyTakeaway: Philosophical depth and Swiss independence fostered Einstein's revolutionary physics.

cards.title: The Architect of Spacetime
cards.summary: Showing an early genius in mathematics and physics, Albert Einstein taught himself calculus by the age of 14. This intense intellectual curiosity and self-taught mastery drove him to challenge the fundamental assumptions of classical mechanics. Then, during his 1905 Annus Mirabilis, he published four groundbreaking papers detailing the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and the mass-energy equivalence formula E = mc2. These revolutionary theories permanently transformed the scientific understanding of space, time, mass, and energy, earning him global recognition and the Nobel Prize. By 1915, he had developed the general theory of relativity, extending mechanics to incorporate gravitation and introducing the cosmological constant. Ultimately, Einstein's 1905 breakthroughs and relativity theories revolutionized modern physics.
cards.readerQuestion: What breakthroughs established Einstein as the world's leading theoretical physicist?
cards.keyTakeaway: Einstein's revolutionary theories permanently transformed our understanding of the universe.

cards.title: Exile of the Century
cards.summary: In 1933, Albert Einstein fled Nazi Germany during a visit to the United States to escape escalating persecution . The rapid rise of Adolf Hitler forced the physicist to abandon his homeland for permanent American safety . He signed a critical letter to President Franklin D. Roosevelt in 1939 warning of German nuclear weapons . This urgent warning alerted the American government to atomic threats, forever altering global military history . Decades earlier, in 1917, Einstein published a groundbreaking paper introducing the concept of stimulated emission . Ultimately, political exile forced this legendary thinker to reshape both scientific progress and global warfare .
cards.readerQuestion: How did global political turmoil force Einstein to flee his homeland and alter history?
cards.keyTakeaway: Political exile forced Einstein to reshape scientific and military history.

cards.title: The Reluctant Quantum Rebel
cards.summary: Albert Einstein spent his final decades trying to disprove the fundamental randomness of quantum mechanics . This stubborn crusade stemmed from his deep philosophical conviction that nature operates under strict deterministic laws . He fiercely opposed the introduction of probability and indeterminacy as fundamental properties of physical reality . Consequently, he spent his final years isolated from the mainstream physics community . His final years were dedicated to an unsuccessful attempt at formulating a unified field theory . Ultimately, Einstein rejected quantum randomness, choosing isolation to defend determinism .
cards.readerQuestion: Why did Einstein spend his final years fighting the scientific mainstream?
cards.keyTakeaway: Einstein rejected quantum randomness, choosing isolation to defend determinism.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: At just twelve years old, the boy discovered an original proof of the Pythagorean theorem entirely on his own .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 1: At just twelve years old, Einstein discovered an original proof of the Pythagorean theorem entirely on his own.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: He taught himself calculus by age 14 .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 3: Albert Einstein showed early genius in mathematics and physics, teaching himself calculus by age 14.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: At age sixteen, Einstein failed the general portion of his entrance exam for the Zurich polytechnic school .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 1: Einstein failed the general portion of his entrance exam for the Zurich polytechnic school at age sixteen, though he excelled in math and physics.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: The teenager renounced his German citizenship in 1896 at age seventeen .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 2: Einstein renounced his German citizenship in 1896 at age seventeen, remaining stateless for five years until acquiring Swiss citizenship in 1901.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: Decades later, during his 1905 Annus Mirabilis, he published four groundbreaking papers detailing the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and mass-energy equivalence .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 3: During his 1905 Annus Mirabilis, he published four groundbreaking papers detailing the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, special relativity, and the mass-energy equivalence formula E = mc2.

briefSummaryProvenance.sentence: He developed the general theory of relativity in 1915 .
briefSummaryProvenance.fact: Chapter 3: He developed the general theory of relativity in 1915, extending mechanics to incorporate gravitation and introducing the cosmological constant.
